01 Engine Internals — The Newsroom That Runs Your Code

← Back to lessons

Before you write expert JavaScript, you need to see the machine it runs on. Not the physical hardware — the virtual machine inside V8 that parses, compiles, and executes your code.

The Mental Model: A Newsroom

Imagine a newsroom with very specific rules:

  • The Editor (Call Stack) — Can only work on one story at a time. When a new task arrives, it goes on top of the pile. The editor finishes the top story before returning to the one underneath. This is why JavaScript is "single-threaded."
  • The Filing Cabinet (Heap) — Where all the data lives: objects, arrays, closures. No structure, no order — just a massive storage area. The editor grabs references to things stored here.
  • The Assignment Desk (Event Loop) — Constantly checks: "Is the editor free? Is there work waiting?" If yes, it hands the next task to the editor. The assignment desk never does work itself — it just routes.
  • The Wire Services (Web APIs / Node APIs) — External systems that handle slow work: network requests, timers, file I/O. The editor says "call me back when you have the story" and keeps working on other things.

This is the entire runtime model. Every JavaScript behavior you've ever been confused by — why setTimeout(fn, 0) doesn't run immediately, why a long loop freezes the UI, why Promises resolve before timeouts — traces back to this architecture.

The Call Stack

The call stack is a LIFO (last-in, first-out) data structure. Every time you call a function, a new "frame" is pushed onto the stack. When the function returns, the frame is popped off.

function multiply(a, b) {
  return a * b;
}

function square(n) {
  return multiply(n, n);
}

function printSquare(n) {
  const result = square(n);
  console.log(result);
}

printSquare(4);

Here's what the call stack looks like at each step:

// Step 1: printSquare(4) is called
[printSquare]

// Step 2: square(4) is called inside printSquare
[printSquare, square]

// Step 3: multiply(4, 4) is called inside square
[printSquare, square, multiply]

// Step 4: multiply returns 16 → popped off
[printSquare, square]

// Step 5: square returns 16 → popped off
[printSquare]

// Step 6: console.log(16) runs, printSquare returns → empty
[]

Stack overflow happens when you push more frames than the stack can hold — usually from infinite recursion. The name "Stack Overflow" (the website) is literally this concept.

Why Single-Threaded Matters

JavaScript has ONE call stack. One. That means it can do exactly one thing at a time. In the newsroom analogy: one editor, one story at a time.

This is why a long-running while loop freezes the browser. The editor is stuck on one story and can't process clicks, scrolls, or renders. The UI thread is the same thread as your JavaScript.

// This will freeze the browser for ~5 seconds
const start = Date.now();
while (Date.now() - start < 5000) {
  // Blocking the call stack
}
console.log("Finally done");
// During those 5 seconds, NOTHING else can happen

The Heap

The heap is where objects and functions are allocated in memory. Unlike the stack (which is structured and ordered), the heap is unstructured — it's just a big pool of memory.

const user = { name: "Trevor", role: "journalist" };
const tags = ["JS", "TS", "React"];

When you write this, the variable names (user, tags) live on the stack as references. The actual objects live in the heap. This distinction matters for understanding:

  • Pass by reference — Objects are passed as heap references, not copies
  • Garbage collection — V8 periodically sweeps the heap to free unreachable objects
  • Memory leaks — Happen when objects in the heap can never be garbage-collected because something still references them

The Event Loop

The event loop is the most misunderstood part of JavaScript. Here's the precise algorithm:

  1. Is the call stack empty?
  2. If yes, check the microtask queue. Run ALL microtasks until the queue is empty.
  3. Then check the macrotask queue. Run ONE macrotask.
  4. Go back to step 1.

Microtasks always run before macrotasks. This is why Promise.then() runs before setTimeout(fn, 0) even though both are "asynchronous." Promises schedule microtasks. setTimeout schedules macrotasks.

Microtasks vs Macrotasks

Microtasks (high priority)
Promise.then()
Promise.catch()
Promise.finally()
queueMicrotask()
MutationObserver
Macrotasks (normal priority)
setTimeout()
setInterval()
setImmediate() (Node)
requestAnimationFrame()
I/O callbacks
UI rendering

The Classic Interview Question

console.log("1");

setTimeout(() => {
  console.log("2");
}, 0);

Promise.resolve().then(() => {
  console.log("3");
});

console.log("4");

// Output: 1, 4, 3, 2

Why this order?

  1. "1" — synchronous, runs immediately on the call stack
  2. setTimeout — schedules "2" as a macrotask
  3. Promise.then — schedules "3" as a microtask
  4. "4" — synchronous, runs immediately
  5. Call stack is now empty. Event loop checks microtasks: "3" runs.
  6. Microtask queue is empty. Event loop grabs one macrotask: "2" runs.

V8's Compilation Pipeline

JavaScript is not "interpreted." Modern engines like V8 use Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation — a hybrid approach:

  1. Parser reads your source code and produces an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)
  2. Ignition (V8's interpreter) converts the AST to bytecode and starts executing immediately. This is fast startup.
  3. TurboFan (V8's optimizing compiler) watches which functions are "hot" (called frequently) and compiles them to optimized machine code.
  4. If TurboFan's assumptions are wrong (e.g., a function always received numbers, but now gets a string), it deoptimizes — throws away the optimized code and falls back to Ignition.

Why this matters for your code: V8 optimizes based on consistent types. If a function always receives numbers, TurboFan generates fast numeric code. If you suddenly pass a string, it deoptimizes. This is why TypeScript's consistent typing isn't just for developer experience — it also helps V8 generate faster code.

Hidden Classes and Inline Caches

V8 creates "hidden classes" (also called shapes or maps) for your objects. Objects with the same property names, added in the same order, share a hidden class.

// These two objects share a hidden class
const a = { x: 1, y: 2 };
const b = { x: 3, y: 4 };

// This object has a DIFFERENT hidden class (different property order)
const c = { y: 2, x: 1 };

// This creates a new hidden class (extra property)
a.z = 3;

Performance insight: Create objects with the same properties in the same order. Avoid adding properties after creation. This isn't premature optimization — it's how V8 was designed to work. Think of it like a style guide for the newsroom: consistency makes everything faster.

Garbage Collection

V8 uses a generational garbage collector with two main areas:

  • Young generation (nursery) — Small, fast. Most objects die young. Collected frequently with a "scavenge" algorithm.
  • Old generation — Objects that survive multiple young-generation collections are promoted here. Collected less often with a "mark-and-sweep" algorithm.

The mental model: most objects are like breaking news — relevant for minutes, then forgotten. A few become reference material and move to the archives.

Common Memory Leak Patterns

// 1. Forgotten event listeners
element.addEventListener('click', handler);
// If you remove the element but not the listener,
// the handler (and everything it closes over) stays in memory

// 2. Closures holding references
function createLeak() {
  const hugeArray = new Array(1000000).fill('data');
  return function() {
    // This closure keeps hugeArray alive forever
    console.log(hugeArray.length);
  };
}
const leak = createLeak();
// hugeArray can never be collected as long as leak exists

// 3. Global variables
window.cache = {};
// Global references are never garbage-collected

Knowledge Check

1. What will this print, and why?

setTimeout(() => console.log("A"), 0);
queueMicrotask(() => console.log("B"));
console.log("C");
C, B, A. "C" is synchronous (runs first). "B" is a microtask (runs before macrotasks when the stack is empty). "A" is a macrotask (setTimeout), so it runs last.

2. Why does V8 "deoptimize" a function?

TurboFan compiles optimized machine code based on assumptions about types. If a function that always received numbers suddenly receives a string, the optimized code can't handle it. V8 throws away the optimized version and falls back to the interpreter (Ignition) to handle the new type.

3. Why is JavaScript single-threaded but non-blocking?

Single-threaded: one call stack, one thing executing at a time. Non-blocking: async operations (network, timers, I/O) are delegated to Web APIs / Node APIs that run on separate threads. When they complete, their callbacks are queued for the event loop to pick up. The main thread never waits.

Exercise: Predict the Event Loop

Without running this code, predict the exact output order. Then run it in the browser console to verify.

console.log("start");

setTimeout(() => console.log("timeout 1"), 0);
setTimeout(() => console.log("timeout 2"), 0);

Promise.resolve()
  .then(() => {
    console.log("promise 1");
    queueMicrotask(() => console.log("microtask inside promise"));
  })
  .then(() => console.log("promise 2"));

queueMicrotask(() => console.log("microtask 1"));

console.log("end");

Bonus: Draw the call stack, microtask queue, and macrotask queue at each step. If you can trace this accurately, you understand the event loop.
